perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information : has the meaning given in clause 2 of this Agreement; Disclosing Party : a Party to this Agreement which discloses its Confidential Information to the other Party; Purpose : [[INSERT DETAILS e.g. to establish a business relationship in respect to the supply of [named service provision or goods] from X, etc]]; Receiving Party : a Party to this Agreement which receives the Confidential Information from the other P arty; Representatives : any employees, officers, directors, professional advisors or consultants in relation to each Party and any of its Affiliates, who are actively and directly engaged in the Purpose; Term : has the meaning given in clause 10.1 of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information : excludes any data or information, regardless of format, shared during discussions; Disclosing Party : a Party that believes it is sharing confidential data; Purpose : ill-defined and subject to either party's interpretation; Receiving Party : a Party that may or may not be in receipt of actual confidential information; Representatives : individuals loosely associated with either party; Term : subject to the discretion of NSK Europe Limited.
perturbation.explanation: By redefining 'Confidential Information' to exclude all shared data, the contract becomes self-defeating. Additionally, the modified definitions of 'Purpose', 'Receiving Party', 'Representatives', and 'Term' create ambiguity, rendering the core obligations unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets (as regulated by Directive (EU) 2016/943 and national implementations, e.g., UK's Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018)
perturbation.law_explanation: The EU Directive and UK Regulations define trade secrets and confidential information with specific criteria for protection. By stating that 'Confidential Information' excludes all data, the contract actively undermines these protections and allows for misappropriation of trade secrets, conflicting with the Directive's aim to harmonize trade secret protection across the EU.

This item of legislation is currently only available in its original format. Interpretation This section has no associated Explanatory Memorandum 2. For the purposes of these Regulations— “court” means— (a) in England and Wales, a County Court hearing centre where there is also a Chancery District Registry or the High Court (as provided for in rule 63.13 of the Civil Procedure Rules 1998( 1 )), (b) in Scotland, the sheriff or the Court of Session, and (c) in Northern Ireland, a county court or the High Court; “infringer” means a person who has unlawfully acquired, used or disclosed a trade secret; “infringing goods” means goods, the design, functioning, production process, marketing or a characteristic of which significantly benefits from a trade secret unlawfully acquired, used or disclosed; “trade secret” means information which— (a) is secret in the sense that it is not, as a body or in the precise configuration and assembly of its components, generally known among, or readily accessible to, persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question, (b) has commercial value because it is secret, and (c) has been subject to reasonable steps under the circumstances, by the person lawfully in control of the information, to keep it secret; “trade secret holder” means any person lawfully controlling a trade secret. ( 1 ) S.I. 1998/3132 ; relevant amending instruments are S.I. 2009/2092 , Schedule 1, S.I. 2009/3390 , rule 38(a), S.I. 2013/1974 , rule 26(g), and S.I. 2014/407 , rule 27(a). Previous: Provision Next: Provision Back to top Options/Help Print Options Print The Whole Instrument PDF The Who
perturbation.justification: The modified contract contradicts the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018. The original contract defines 'Confidential Information' with reference to clause 2. The changed contract redefines 'Confidential Information' to exclude all shared data, and redefines key terms to be ambiguous. This contradicts the regulation's definition of 'trade secret', specifically the requirement that the information '(a) is secret...generally known among, or readily accessible to, persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question, (b) has commercial value because it is secret, and (c) has been subject to reasonable steps under the circumstances, by the person lawfully in control of the information, to keep it secret'. By excluding all shared data, the modified contract fails to protect trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction, and fitting the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.  CONFIDENTIALITY OBLIGATIONS 3.1. In consideration of the Disclosing Party disclosing Confidential Information to the Receiving Party, the Receiv ing Party undertakes that it shall: a) keep the Confidential Inf ormation secret and confidential, using at least the same degree of care as its uses to protect its own confidential information but no less than a reasonable degree of care ;
perturbation.changed_text: 3.  CONFIDENTIALITY OBLIGATIONS 3.1. In consideration of the Disclosing Party disclosing what *it deems* Confidential Information to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party undertakes that it shall: a) keep the 'Confidential Information' subject to its internal review processes, with no obligation to keep the information secret or confidential, and may share the information as it sees fit.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the confidentiality obligation. It allows the Receiving Party to disregard the confidential nature of the information and share it as they see fit, conflicting with the basic principle of a non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Consideration and Good Faith
perturbation.law_citation: Implied duty of good faith (common law principle applied in England and Wales)
perturbation.law_explanation: English contract law includes an implied duty of good faith. By allowing the Receiving Party to arbitrarily disregard confidentiality, the modification undermines this duty. The change makes the Disclosing Party's consideration (disclosure of information) worthless, as the Receiving Party is not obligated to protect the information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text fundamentally contradicts the original intent of the confidentiality agreement. The original text mandates keeping 'Confidential Information secret and confidential', while the changed text allows the Receiving Party to disregard the confidential nature and share it as they see fit. The Law Explanation states that by allowing the Receiving Party to arbitrarily disregard confidentiality, the modification undermines the implied duty of good faith. This duty is a principle in English contract law. The edited text negates the Disclosing Party's consideration (disclosure of information), rendering it worthless since the Receiving Party has no obligation to protect it.

perturbation.original_text: 5. RETURN OR DESTRUCTION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 5.1. Upon the Disclosing Party ’s written request , the Receiving Party shall (as requested by the Disclosing Party) either return to the Disclosing Party or destroy (provided that any such destruction shall be confirmed in writing by the Receiving Party) all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party including all copies, reproductions, notes , extracts and summaries which include, reflect, incorporate or otherwise contain the Disclosing Party’s Confidenti al Infor mation whether in tangible form or otherwise, such as electronic mail or computer files .
perturbation.changed_text: 5. RETURN OR DESTRUCTION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 5.1. Upon the Disclosing Party ’s request, *but at the Receiving Party's discretion*, the Receiving Party may or may not return or destroy what *it considers* Confidential Information. No confirmation of destruction is required.
perturbation.explanation: This modification completely nullifies the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The discretionary nature and lack of confirmation render the clause unenforceable and undermines the core purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Enforceability of Contractual Terms
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Contract Law - Certainty of Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: For a contract to be enforceable, its terms must be certain and not vague. This change introduces significant uncertainty, as the obligation to return or destroy information is now entirely discretionary and subject to the Receiving Party's subjective assessment of what constitutes Confidential Information. This lack of certainty makes the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent of the agreement by making the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary, rather than mandatory. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this modification nullifies the obligation, and the Law Explanation highlights the uncertainty this introduces, making the clause unenforceable. Although the scraped law snippets do not provide direct legal support, the explanations clearly highlight that the altered clause deviates significantly from standard contract practices, which aim to protect confidential information, thus suggesting a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 10. TERM AND SURVIVAL OF CONFIDENTIALITY OBLIGATIONS 10.1. The term of this Agreement shall be fo r a period of [three (3) ] years from the Effective Date (the “ Term ”) but may be terminated or extended upon both Parties’ written agreement. 10.2. The rights and obligations contained in this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect for [two (2) ] years after expiration of the Term or termination of this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: 10. TERM AND SURVIVAL OF CONFIDENTIALITY OBLIGATIONS 10.1. The term of this Agreement may be shortened or extended at NSK Europe Limited's sole discretion. 10.2. The rights and obligations contained in this Agreement shall cease immediately upon notice from NSK Europe Limited, regardless of the Term.
perturbation.explanation: This change grants NSK Europe Limited unilateral control over the agreement's term and survival of obligations. This undermines the mutuality of the agreement and creates an imbalance of power.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UK)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 aims to protect parties from unfair contract terms. Granting one party (NSK) the unilateral right to terminate the agreement and extinguish obligations, regardless of the term, is likely to be considered an unreasonable term that attempts to exclude or restrict liability. Section 3 of the Act addresses contract terms that allow a party to render a contractual performance substantially different from what was reasonably expected, therefore, this change is illegal.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the principle of fairness and mutuality as suggested by the Law Explanation, which refers to the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977. The original text provided a balanced term and survival of obligations, while the changed text grants NSK Europe Limited unilateral control, allowing them to shorten or extend the agreement and terminate obligations at their sole discretion. This contradicts the intent of Section 3 of the Act, which aims to prevent one party from rendering contractual performance substantially different from what was reasonably expected. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't provide the full context of the Act, it does confirm the existence of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, reinforcing the legal basis for the claim that the change is illegal. The imbalance of power created by the modification undermines the fairness of the contract, fitting the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition as it deviates from legally recognized fairness and mutuality, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
